EXHIBIT 1

 
Page 2
1 Friday, September 4, 2009 9:30 a.m.
2
3 THE COURT: We're on the record.
4 Good morning, counsel.
5 MR. ROGERS: Good morning, Your Honor.
6 THE COURT: Please call the case.
7 THE CLERK: Calling the cases of Marshal Enderby versus
8 California Public Utilities Commission, Case No. CGC-07-464877,
9 and companion case Alok Kumar versus California PUC, Case
10 No. 480942.
11 Counsel, please state your appearances for the record and in
12 which matter you're appearing.
13 MR. ROGERS: Richard Rogers appearing for the plaintiffs in
14 both matters.
15 MR. LYON: David Lyon appearing for defendants on the motion
16 to consolidate, which I think means I'm appearing in both
17 matters.
18 THE COURT: You say for the defendants, who are the --
19 MR. LYON: Or for the defendant California Public Utilities
20 Commission.
21 MS. RUIZ: Celia Ruiz representing the California Public
22 Utilities Commission.
23 THE COURT: In both matters?
24 MS. RUIZ: In both matters.
25 MR. FANG: Forrest Fang for the California Public Utilities
26 Commission in the Kumar case management conference, but in both
27 matters.
28 THE COURT: Are you representing the PUC in both matters?

 
Page 3
1 MR. FANG: Well, in the -- I'm appearing only in the, I'm
2 sorry, the Kumar.
3 THE COURT: Well, there's two cases.
4 MR. FANG: Yes, the Kumar.
5 THE COURT: You're only in Kumar?
6 MR. FANG: Yes, that's correct.
7 THE COURT: And Ms. Ruiz?
8 MS. RUIZ: I'm going to be trial counsel on both matters,
9 but I am here --
10 THE COURT: Are you here today on both matters?
11 MS. RUIZ: The Enderby, the Enderby case management
12 conference.
13 THE COURT: Well, I'm starting with the motion first.
14 MS. RUIZ: Mr. Lyon then from our office will be arguing the
15 motion.
16 THE COURT: Let me just start over. I just want to be clear
17 who is representing the PUC here today and in which case. I
18 know you're all representing the PUC in something, but I'm not
19 clear whether all three of you are -- aren't you all with the
20 same law firm?
21 MS. RUIZ: We are.
22 THE COURT: Well, then isn't the law firm representing the
23 PUC in both cases, and doesn't it follow from that that each of
24 you represents the PUC in both cases?
25 MS. RUIZ: Exactly.
26 THE COURT: Okay. All right. We're here today on
27 defendant's motion to consolidate in both of the cases, and you
28 can proceed with the argument if you wish to argue it.

 
Page 4
1 MR. LYON: Okay. Good morning, Your Honor.
2 THE COURT: Good morning.
3 MR. LYON: I would like to briefly outline the arguments we
4 raised in our brief, and of course I'm happy to address any
5 questions that you might have.
6 We have brought a motion to consolidate the action of
7 Enderby and others versus the PUC, as well as the Kumar versus
8 puC case for several different reasons. The primary reason is
9 that both cases raise overlapping claims of age discrimination
10 against the Public Utilities Commission. In fact, there is a
11 core set of claims that were copied verbatim from the Enderby
12 action and that are re-asserted in the Kumar action, and these
13 claims are essential to the age discrimination claims. We set
14 them forth in our reply brief in footnote 2. These claims
15 include the allegations that the CPUC claimed that its workforce
16 was aging and needed to hire younger employees; the allegation
17 that the CPUC has implemented policies, practices, and
18 procedures to promote or hire younger employees over older
19 employees with greater knowledge, skills, and abilities; it
20 includes the allegation that we have promoted a statistically
21 significant lower number of older employees than would be
22 expected; it includes the allegation that our allegedly age
23 discriminatory policies permeate all of our divisions and
24 departments; and it includes the allegation that we have adopted
25 a variety of practices to effectuate this alleged policy of
26 discrimination. Those allegations are central to the age
27 discrimination claims in both of these actions.
28 THE COURT: Well, when was the latest of the two filed?

 
Page 5
1 MR. LYON: The latest of the two filed was the Kumar action.
2 THE COURT: When?
3 MR. LYON: That was filed I believe in the fall of 2007, if
4 I'm correct.
5 THE COURT: Is there anything that you have learned that is
6 relevant to this motion that you didn't know in February of this
7 year?
8 MR. LYON: We were only recently brought into this case in
9 December.
10 THE COURT: You were brought in December of ‘08.
11 MR. LYON: Right.
12 THE COURT: So I'm asking you is there anything that has
13 occurred in this case since February of this year that is
14 relevant to this motion which is now being made in August for a
15 hearing in September?
16 MR. LYON: We immediately began working on both cases,
17 which, of course, both of which raise substantial allegations
18 allegedly on behalf of a number of claimants. We immediately
19 commenced working on a variety of discovery issues in the
20 Enderby action. The Kumar case was not proceeding rapidly, and
21 we were devoting substantial energy to getting up to speed in
22 the Enderby action and then analyzing it. As we proceeded, we
23 recognized the striking overlap of these cases became
24 increasingly apparent.
25 THE COURT: You mean between December of last year and now
26 it has taken you that long to realize what you're now telling
27 us, that these actions appear to be similar or overlapping?
28 Would not, whatever you're asserting now about the overlap,

 
Page 6
1 wasn't that apparent from the face of the pleadings, because
2 isn't your argument here that the pleadings are essentially
3 carbon copies of each other except they relate to different
4 positions?
5 MR. LYON: Right, they are apparent from the face of the
6 pleadings. And I think the Court is beginning to focus on the
7 idea of whether or not an initial trial date scheduled in the
8 Enderby action should be allowed to be postponed and the timing
9 of raising of this issue. The fact of the matter is that the
10 Enderby action should have never been split off from the Kumar
11 action. And if you look at the history of these two cases, if
12 you look at the complaint that's still on file in the Enderby
13 action, it in fact includes the age discrimination claims which
14 are being alleged in Kumar.
15 What happened is that the plaintiffs only sought a limited
16 class certification in the Enderby action and then following
17 that limited certification then filed this -- the Kumar action,
18 which in part attempted to assert the age discrimination claims
19 on behalf of class members who were excluded from class
20 certification in Enderby, and so the fact of the matter is that
21 the Enderby action should never have been split off to begin
22 with.
23 THE COURT: Wasn't there an understanding between the Court
24 and counsel dating back many, many months that the Enderby
25 action would relate to the specific jobs that were the subject
26 of the motion to certify, and, in fact, didn't all of that start
27 when there was some discovery dispute over whether the plaintiff
28 would be allowed to go into other job positions, and wasn't all

 
Page 7
1 of that discussed and made clear long, long ago and the case set
2 for trial in February with respect to these job positions, for
3 which there had been certification of a class?
4 MR. LYON: Your Honor, at the time, there was a discussion
5 regarding management of discovery, and then even up to the time
6 of the class certification order there was no Kumar action on
7 file. The Kumar action was only put on file after this Court
8 had issued its class certification and effectively it's sort of
9 an end run around that.
10 If the plaintiffs had wanted to broaden the class they had
11 sought in Enderby to assert the very same or substantially
12 similar age discrimination claims, they could have brought a
13 motion asking this Court to revisit the class certification
14 issue; that would have been the proper mechanism, and the effect
15 of doing that would have been that these actions would not have
16 been split apart improperly with all of the serious adverse
17 consequences that that splitting has, but, rather, this case
18 would have proceeded as a consolidated matter, and this motion
19 would not even be necessary.
20 THE COURT: Well, you knew in February of this year that
21 this case was going to trial with respect to these specific jobs
22 in November, right? As a matter of fact, there was an agreement
23 on the record on that issue, wasn't there?
24 MR. LYON: I'm sorry. I'm not sure I know which agreement
25 you're alluding to.
26 THE COURT: When the case -- when we had a CMC in February,
27 this case, it was stated that the case is going to trial in
28 November, and the question was asked does anybody object, or

 
Page 8
1 something like that, and there wasn't any. It was understood,
2 wasn't it, in February of this year, that this case was going to
3 trial in November on these specific jobs; isn't that true?
4 MR. LYON: I actually wasn't an attendant.
5 THE COURT: Well, have you looked at the transcript of the
6 February hearing? Do you have the February transcript?
7 On February 25th, at that hearing, Mr. Rogers says:
8 "We are stipulating that the class will be fixed
9 as of 60 days before the trial, whenever the trial
10 happens. At the moment it's set for November 4,
11 2009, but if it's continued for any reason, it would
12 be 60 days before the continued date just so that we
13 can fix the class members in terms of who is included
14 in any relief.
15 "THE COURT: Okay. Is that a stipulation?
16 "MS. SHUKLA: Yes, Your Honor."
17 MR. LYON: Right.
18 THE COURT: And Mr. Rogers says, "yes."
19 So I repeat the question. Wasn't it understood in February
20 of this year that this case was set for trial in November and it
21 was going forward as it presently is scheduled?
22 MR. LYON: I think the transcript that you just read reveals
23 that there was consideration that that trial date might he
24 moved.
29 THE COURT: But nobody has asked to have it moved, have
26 they?
27 MR. LYON: And that that was the initial trial date.
28 THE COURT: But has anybody asked to have it moved since

 
Page 9
1 February?
2 MR. LYON: Not -- I don't believe that there's been a formal
3 reguest.
4 THE COURT: Okay.
5 MR. LYON: I do believe, Your Honor, that at that meeting --
6 THE COURT: There hasn't been an informal request either of
7 the Court, has there? You say there hasn't been a formal
8 request. Has there been any request?
9 MR. LYON: I believe if consolidation were granted, I don't
10 believe the parties would be prepared to proceed in early
11 November ~--—
12 THE COURT: Well --
13 MR. LYON: -- in a consolidated action.
14 THE COURT: You're saying that your motion to consolidate is
15 really intended to or at least will have the effect of
16 continuing the trial date, so your answer to my question is this
17 ig the first time that anybody has asked to change the trial
18 date, is that correct, or did somebody ask before today for
19 that?
20 MR. LYON: Well, if I may jump back to the CMC that occurred
21 in February. It's my understanding that there was an
22 off-the-record discussion that preceded the on-the-record
23 discussion, and that Marion McWiiliams of our office in fact
24 raised the possibility of a motion to consolidate these actions,
25 so that was considered at that time.
26 THE COURT: In February. And when was the motion filed?
27 MR. LYON: I believe it was filed in July.
28 THE COURT: Well, it was considered in February and then

 
Page 10

1 five months later it's made?

2 MR. LYON: <I think if you look at the record of what's been
3 happening in this case is that the parties have been extremely
4 busy conducting a lot of discovery. There's been a tremendous
5 amount of document discovery work done.

6 THE COURT: Has there been any discovery in the Kumar case?
7 MR. LYON: It's my understanding that -- I know that the

8 plaintiffs were asking for a stay of that action.

9 THE COURT: Has there been any discovery in the Kumar case?
10 MR. LYON: And there's been some discovery propounded. We
11 had some objections, and we have been unable to proceed with any
12 meet and confer meeting and conference with the plaintiffs'

13 counsel in order to resolve that issue.
14 THE COURT: Has any information been provided by either side
15 to the other in discovery in the Kumar case? That's a yes-or-no

16 question.

17 MR. LYON: May I check?

18 THE COURT: Yes.

19 (pause in proceedings.)

20 MR. LYON: We believe that there was some interrogatory
21 responses provided in the spring of this year in the Kumar
22 action.

23 THE COURT: Responses by whom?

24 MR. LYON: By the defendant.

25 THE COURT: You're representing that that happened?

26 MR. LYON: That's --

27 MR. FANG: Your Honor, it's my recollection that there were
28 some responses that Ms. Shukla had provided through our offices

 
Page il
1 in February, I believe it was in the Kumar case, but I would
2 have to double check that. I believe that there may have also
3 been a limited number of documents that were also produced.
4 THE COURT: Well, Mr. Rogers can address that as well.
5 But let me say this, Mr. Lyon, you say how busy the parties
6 have been doing discovery, and then I ask you whether there was
7 any discovery in Kumar and you seem to be very uncertain about
8 it, and if there was any it seems to be very minimal, so I would
9 understand your prior answer that there was an awful lot of hard
10 work being done on discovery to refer to discovery to get ready
11 for the November trial.
12 MR. LYON: I know that there was a lot of discovery being
13 done for the Enderby case, yes.
14 THE COURT: For the trial in November, right, so that's the
15 thrust of your comments that the parties have been working very
16 hard doing discovery, it is discovery for the trial in Kumar in
17 November, right?
18 MR. LYON: Obviously, this Court has set an initial trial
19 date for the Enderby action in November, that's -- yes.
20 THE COURT: But I want to get the answer to my guestion,
21 which is when you tell me that there's been a lot of discovery
22 done, the parties have been working hard doing discovery. My
23 question is that discovery was for the November trial in
24 Enderby, correct?
25 MR. LYON: The discovery would have to be done no matter
26 what the trial date, so it wasn't -~-
27 THE COURT: You know, I'm not asking whether the discovery
28 would have to be done for the trial date, the discovery was done

 
Page 12
1 in connection with the matter now set for trial in November in
2 Enderby, yes or no?
3 MR. LYON: Yes, that's true.
4 THE COURT: Okay.
5 MR. LYON: Okay. If I can focus, the Court does seem to be
6 focusing on the idea of the initial trial date that's set for
7 November, and as I noted, if this matter proceeded through a
8 proper motion to enlarge the class, this consolidation motion
9 would be unnecessary. If the class had been enlarged in a
10 proper matter, there would have been consideration as the case
11 proceeded into how to -- into the discovery that an enlarged
12 class would require and whether there would be any effect on
13 other scheduling, including the trial date.
14 In addition to the overlap of claims, of course, there's an
15 overlapping of plaintiffs as well, and we note that how the
16 Kumar named plaintiffs actually make allegations in their
17 complaint that indicate that they would be claimants in the
18 Enderby action. There are serious consequences from this
19 duplication, and these consequences are more significant, we
20 believe, than moving of an initial trial date and what we
21 consider to be an improperly split off action. First, of
22 course, there's going to be duplication, which is inefficient
23 and wasteful and a burden on all the parties, the Court, as well
24 as other litigants hoping to have access to this Court.
25 THE COURT: Well, these named plaintiffs want to go to
26 trial, so...
27 MR. LYON: While that raises an issue regarding, to the
28 extent -- well, the named plaintiffs, and then there are also

 
Page 13
1 the claimants and whether those claimants were properly given
2 notice that their participation in this action would impair any
3 rights that they might otherwise have in the Kumar action.
4 THE COURT: Well, are you concerned that they might be
5 giving up rights against the PUC?
6 MR. LYON: Well, it's a question of the propriety of notice.
7 And we're facing a situation where, as the PUC, we do not want
8 to face serial trials. We do not want to face one-way
9 application of collateral estoppel where basically the same
10 claimants sort of gives it a shot in the Enderby case as it's
11 currently structured.
12 THE COURT: I think Mr. Rogers said in his papers that he's
13 basically not considering arguing collateral estoppel.
14 MR. LYON: I don't know if that was the formal stipulation
15 or agreement by the plaintiffs.
16 THE COURT: Okay. Well, he said what he said.
17 MR. LYON: Right. It still raises the issue as to why there
18 should be a needless duplication of consideration of the same
19 factual issues which arises from the fact of the identity of
20 substantial claims from the two actions.
21 Additionally, consolidation would likely -- would improve
22 the efficiency and effectiveness of the two actions combined
23 together, and thus would still hold advantage to the named
24 plaintiffs and claimants to the extent they have claims in both
25 actions.
26 THE COURT: Incidentally, what he says, just so the record
27 is clear, he says in his brief, at page 4:
28 "We are talking about totally different promotion

 
Page 14
1 decisions at different times with different decision
2 makers. Plaintiffs know of no authority for the
3 application of collateral estoppel in these
4 circumstances."
5 MR. LYON: Right. What he ignores when doing that is the
6 fact that there are substantial claims, allegations which will
7 have to -- which will be subject to adjudication which are
8 verbatim identical claims, and what these claims relate to is
9 the alleged discriminatory intent or impact of opening up the
10 CPUC promotions to outsiders, changes in tests designed to
11 facilitate opening that up, and then a variety of other policies
12 and practices.
13 The fact of the matter is that as those allegations have
14 been stated, they are not particular to each promotion, rather,
15 that they're based upon decisions that were made back in 2000
16 and 2001 in part and other policies and procedures that were
17 allegedly put in place after that, and those are the very
18 same -- those are the very same changes, and that it's going to
19 be an identical inquiry in the two actions. And when you have
20 that duplication --
21 THE COURT: Well, Mr. Rogers is saying it's an identical
22 inquiry in the Enderby action, because he doesn't propose to try
23 the cases of individuals, but you come back and you say, well,
24 the case is going to last for a long time because you want to
25 put in evidence about hiring of individuals. He's saying
26 exactly the opposite for the Enderby case. He's saying I'm
27 going to put on class-wide evidence. I don't intend to get into
28 the issues about the individual applications and individual

 
Page 15
1 promotions, except perhaps for some anecdotal testimony.
2 But, as I understand it, his position is, in the trial, it's
3 going to be a trial that relates class-wide to the class that
4 has been determined, and I thought that you were coming back and
5 saying, oh, but you're entitled to put in evidence about
6 individual applications and individual rejections and promotions
7 and the like.
8 MR. LYON: ZI think the assertion that somehow the trial on
9 Enderby is only going to be limited to class allegations is
10 inconsistent with what you just read that the plaintiffs stated
11 in their opposition, emphasizing the variety of individual
12 inquiries that are going to be required.
13 THE COURT: Say that again.
14 MR. LYON: You just quoted from the plaintiffs' pleading,
15 noting the -- in asserting that collateral estoppel wouldn't be
16 available, you're saying that plaintiffs appear to be conceding
17 that, and that emphasizes --
18 THE COURT: He says for different jobs. As I understand it,
19 he's saying for the different jobs that are involved in the
20 other action, that would involve different promotion decisions
21 with different decision makers.
22 My understanding is that the claim that he intends to try is
23 a claim of commonality with respect to the several positions
24 that have been certified. In other words, my understanding is
25 he does not intend to present evidence about at least a large
26 number of individual hiring and promotion decisions, but,
27 rather, to paint with a broader brush that is usually used in
28 class actions.

 
Page 16
1 MR. LYON: Well, in order to prove his claims, because of
2 the proof requirements for age discrimination in relation to
3 causation in relation, of course, to damages, there's going to
4 be individual inquiries which are going to be required, and to
5 the extent that he's -- that you assert that he's going to be
6 making some common claims --
7 THE COURT: Well, this is my understanding of what he
8 intends to do. He can speak for himself, if we give him the
9 chance here in a view minutes.
10 MR. LYON: But then that raises the question as to whether
11 these Enderby plaintiffs and the other claimants are going to be
12 asserting any collateral estoppel from those common inquiries
13 that you just identified.
14 THE COURT: Well, my understanding of the comment that he
15 made, and he can speak for himself, is that he says I know of no
16 authorities that would allow for him to argue collateral
17 estoppel with respect to other job positions than those that
18 have been certified; that's my understanding of what that says.
19 MR. LYON: Right. But the problem is that there's going to
20 be a factual inquiry in the Enderby trial in November regarding
21 these allegations, which are common, which are verbatim from the
22 two actions, and that raises -- I mean, for him to say that he
23 currently knows of no authorities is really a far cry from him
24 stipulating that he's not going to attempt to assert collateral
25 estoppel, and it certainly doesn't provide the defendant the
26 protection that it should be granted in that connection.
27 And California public policy consistently emphasizes that
28 related actions should be consolidated, and it emphasizes the

 
Page 17
1 concerns about keeping initial trial dates is an important
2 factor, but it's only one of many factors that needs to be
3 considered, and that courts --~
4 THE COURT: But if your point is that the process, the
5 testing and the mechanism and the process is the same for other
6 groups other than the three that have been certified, then that
7 issue would be tried in the Enderby trial, wouldn't it, I mean,
8 and you've been spending months getting ready for that trial.
9 In other words, if it is true that there's no difference between
10 Enderby and the other cases insofar as the conduct of the PUC,
11 then as scheduled now there would be a trial on that subject,
12 wouldn't there be?
13 MR. LYON: Well, the fact of --
14 THE COURT: As I understand it, what you're saying is that
15 it's all one ball of wax, and, if it is, if that were true,
16 Mr. Rogers says it's not, but if that were true, that would be
17 the subject for which you've been getting ready for trial and
18 working so hard in all this discovery that you tell me about
19 anyway; isn't that so?
20 MR. LYON: Well, there are a couple points on that. First,
21 there are some common claims, which is the basis for what the
22 Court looks at when considering consolidation, and then there's
23 the second issue that we noted as well in terms of the disparate
24 impact and how to properly prove disparate impact, and the cases
25 are very consistent that you don't show disparate impact just by
26 showing an impact upon the class of people you happen to
27 represent, but you need to show a disparate impact upon everyone
28 who has been adversely -- who has been affected by the policy

 
Page 18
1 that you claim is discriminatory, and that's -- that inquiry
2 will need to be an agency-wide inquiry, and the plaintiffs are
3 now telling us that they're not prepared to make that showing.
4 THE COURT: Well, he's taking the position he doesn't have
5 to make that showing.
6 MR. LYON: Right. And his position -- what his position
7 does --
8 THE COURT: Well, I mean, if he's right, then your argument
9 is not sound, and if he's wrong, he's going to and his clients
10 are going to suffer the consequences of it.
11 MR. LYON: Well, his position basically serves to maximize
12 the possibility that there are going to be inconsistent results
13 in the Enderby action and in the Kumar action, because he is
14 effectively saying I am entitled to invoke some specialized
15 burden or -- I'm entitled to use some specialized showing for
16 Enderby that would not apply to Kumar, which means that that
17 is -- just simply encourages reaching inconsistent results on
18 the same factual inquiries and encourages incurring more
19 duplication.
20 THE COURT: Well, both cases are in this Court, I mean,
21 what's the danger of the inconsistent results? How are we going
22 to get inconsistent results? Are we going to have different
23 juries and different results in the two cases or what? In other
24 words, if the law does not permit what you call inconsistent
25 results, then presumably that would be a legal issue that would
26 be addressed by me. If the law does permit these results to be
27 different, then I guess they're not inconsistent and they're
28 lawful, but both cases are in the same court.

 
Page 19
1 MR. LYON: Right. Whether this Court permits the
2 inconsistent results or not, there's no need --
3 THE COURT: Well, it's whether the law permits it, really.
4 MR. LYON: Yes, there's no need to incur that risk, that
5 cost, and, frankly, it makes a little sense to have duplicative
6 inquiries by one court reaching inconsistent results on the same
7 factual contentions.
8 THE COURT: Would the inconsistent results, if we have them
9 as you described them, would they be lawful or not lawful?
10 MR. LYON: Well, that's a really complicated inquiry, and we

1 include the reasoning.
2 MR. LYON: Okay.
3 THE COURT: Okay. Defendant has moved for consolidation in
4 the following two cases: Enderby versus CPUC and Kumar versus
5 cpuc. The instant action, Enderby, was filed on July 5, 2007,
6 was certified as a class action on July 16, 2008, and is set for
7 trial in November of this year. The Kumar case was filed on
8 October 15, 2008, and there has been virtually no discovery in
9 that action, or at the least very minimal discovery, and there
10 has been no motion filed in that action for or against class
11 certification. Plaintiffs in both cases allege that the CPUC
12 has engaged in a pattern and practice of discrimination in
13 promotions, but the allegations in Enderby are limited to age
14 discrimination whereas the allegations in Kumar encompass
15 discrimination based on race and national origin discrimination
16 as well as age. Moreover, the Enderby class, as certified, was
17 limited to plaintiffs who have applied for at least one of three
18 positions: PURA-IV, PURA-V, and P&PS. The Kumar action applies
19 to all other positions except for those three covered in
20 Enderby.
21 Defendant argues that these actions are so interrelated that
22 they warrant consolidation in order to avoid duplicative
23 inquiries, inconsistent findings, and unfairness in allowing
24 plaintiffs the opportunity to re-litigate similar issues in
25 separate trials. However, this Court agrees with plaintiffs
26 that significant issues of prejudice and timeliness strongly and
27 persuasively counsel against consolidation of the two cases. As
28 explained in Weil and Brown, Civil Procedure Before Trial,

 
Page 33

1 (Rutter Group), section 12:362, and the cases cited therein,

2 these two factors are key to the determination of whether cases
3 should be consolidated.

4 Plaintiffs in the Enderby action are prepared to go to trial
5 in less than three months and so should defendant, especially

6 after more than two full years of discovery and motion practice.
7 The Kumar case, on the other hand, is in the earliest stage of
8 litigation and would likely take one-and-a-half to two years to
9 be prepared for trial, that conclusion being supported by what
10 Mr. Lyon himself said during the oral argument. Thus, if the
11 Court were to consolidate the two actions, the Enderby

12 plaintiffs would be unreasonably delayed for a significant

13 period of time. The prejudice of such delay is exacerbated by
14 the fact that plaintiffs contend that their primary aim is

15 equitable relief in the form of an injunction. Indeed, on

16 January 28th of this year, this Court denied plaintiffs' motion
17 for a preliminary injunction, with one expressed reason, among
18 several, being a lack of urgency because "the Court is willing
19 to set the case for trial in the relatively near future," as

20 stated at page 7, lines 17 and 18 of the hearing transcript.

21 Moreover, defendant has waited until the eleventh hour to move
22 for consolidation of these cases. The Kumar case was filed

23 nearly one year ago, and defense counsel at the time took no

24 action to consolidate the two cases. Defendant's current
25 counsel came on the scene in December of 2008, when the Kumar
26 action was already on file, and yet it was understood in

27 February of this year by all counsel, as well as by the Court,
28 that the Enderby case would go to trial in November of 2009.

 
Page 34
1 That understanding is reflected in the transcript of the Case
2 Management Conference held on February 25, 2009, and the Court
3 referenced it during the argument. Defense counsel delayed in
4 filing a motion to consolidate until July 27, 2009, seven months
5 after counsel were brought into the case and five months after
6 the trial date was set without objection by any counsel. Given
7 all of those circumstances, delaying the trial date in order to
8 consolidate these two actions would be highly prejudicial to the
9 Enderby plaintiffs.
10 Furthermore, contrary to the suggestion made by defendant in
11 its brief, plaintiffs did not make some unfair strategic
12 decision to pursue multiple lawsuits against the defendant, but
13 rather plaintiffs limited the Enderby class to three particular
14 positions because defendant strenuously objected that
15 agency-wide discovery was too burdensome. Thus, it would be
16 incongruous to delay the Enderby plaintiffs’ trial because
17 plaintiffs previously acceded to the accommodation of
18 defendant's convenience by limiting discovery to three
19 positions.
20 Finally, as previously stated, the allegations in the two
21 complaints cover different job positions and indeed different
22 claims, with the Kumar case extending to alleged race and
23 national origin discrimination while the Enderby allegations are
24 limited to age. This strongly undercuts defendant's contentions
25 concerning duplication of litigation and unfairness to the
26 defendant.
27 In sum, due to all of these considerations, the Court finds
28 and concludes that consolidation is not warranted, and it

 
Page 35
1 exercises its discretion in the direction of declining
2 defendant's invitation to consolidate the actions. Defendant's
3 motion in each of the two actions is denied.
4 Having said all that, the Court does believe that the
5 current class members in the Enderby case should receive notice
6 of the kind mentioned by Mr. Rogers during today's argument.
7 That is, the class members should be advised of the pendency of
8 the Kumar case, including a description of the claims in that
9 case and information concerning its status. The class members
10 should also be told that while no determination has been made by
11 the Court on the matter, it is possible that any rights that
12 they may have in connection with the Kumar claims may be lost or
13 otherwise affected if they remain in the Enderby class. And, of
14 course, they should be given the opportunity to opt out of the
15 Enderby class by affirmatively providing notice to that effect.
16 The envelope should reference that it contains matters
17 potentially affecting their rights, just as was done in the
18 class notice, and a return postcard or the like should be
19 provided to make it easy for them to opt out of the Enderby
20 class if they so wish. The same administrator and the same
21 process as was previously used should be used again.
22 Plaintiffs' counsel shall prepare proposed materials to be
23 sent to the class and within -- in making the ruling now with
24 respect to timing matters, I'm willing to listen to what you
25 might say if you want these dates changed in what I'm now going
26 to say, but this is what I propose to rule unless you persuade
27 the Court that we should change these dates.
